topic: Units, Measurement & Dimensions — SI Units, Scalars, Vectors & Significant Figures

questions.text: A light year is a unit of:
questions.explanation: A light year is the distance light travels in one year — approximately 9.46 × 10¹⁵ metres. It is a unit of DISTANCE, not time. This is one of SSC's most repeated traps. The speed of light is 3 × 10⁸ m/s.

questions.text: Which instrument is used to measure the humidity of the atmosphere?
questions.explanation: A hygrometer measures humidity (moisture content) in the atmosphere. A hydrometer measures the density/specific gravity of liquids. A barometer measures atmospheric pressure. A lactometer is a specialised hydrometer to test milk purity.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a scalar quantity?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude (speed) and direction. Mass, temperature, and energy are all scalar quantities (magnitude only, no direction). The vector counterpart of mass is weight; of temperature, there is no vector counterpart.

questions.text: The SI unit of luminous intensity is:
questions.explanation: Candela (cd) is the SI base unit of luminous intensity — the brightness of a light source in a given direction. Lumen (lm) is luminous flux (total light emitted). Lux (lx) is illuminance (lumens per square metre). All are related but measure different aspects of light.

questions.text: A person runs four rounds of a 400-metre circular track. Their total distance and displacement are respectively:
questions.explanation: Distance = total path length = 4 × 400 = 1600 m (scalar, always positive). Displacement = shortest path from start to end. After 4 complete rounds, the person is back at the starting point, so displacement = 0 m. This is a classic scalar/vector distinction trap.

questions.text: Which one of the following pairs has the SAME dimensional formula?
questions.explanation: Work = Force × distance = [ML²T⁻²]. Torque = Force × perpendicular distance = [ML²T⁻²]. Both have the same dimension, though they are physically different concepts. Power = [ML²T⁻³]; Momentum = [MLT⁻¹]; Pressure = [ML⁻¹T⁻²].

questions.text: The unit of which physical quantity is the same as that of impulse?
questions.explanation: Impulse = Force × time = [MLT⁻²] × [T] = [MLT⁻¹]. Momentum = mass × velocity = [M] × [LT⁻¹] = [MLT⁻¹]. Both have the same unit (kg·m/s). This is because impulse equals the change in momentum (Newton's 2nd Law in impulse form: J = Δp).

questions.text: Which of the following is the largest unit of distance?
questions.explanation: 1 Parsec = 3.26 light years = 3.086 × 10¹⁶ m. 1 light year = 9.46 × 10¹⁵ m. 1 AU = 1.496 × 10¹¹ m (Earth-Sun distance). Order from smallest to largest: km < AU < light year < parsec.

questions.text: How many base units does the International System (SI) have?
questions.explanation: The SI has exactly 7 base units: metre (length), kilogram (mass), second (time), ampere (electric current), kelvin (temperature), mole (amount of substance), and candela (luminous intensity). Radian and steradian are supplementary/dimensionless units — not base units.

1 Ångström = 10⁻¹⁰ m = 0.1 nm. It is used to express atomic radii (~1–3 Å), bond lengths, and visible light wavelengths (3800–7500 Å). It is named after Swedish physicist Anders Jonas Ångström. Although not an SI unit, it is widely used in chemistry and spectroscopy.

questions.text: Which of the following instruments is used to measure blood pressure?
questions.explanation: A sphygmomanometer (Greek: sphygmos = pulse) measures blood pressure using an inflatable cuff. It reads systolic and diastolic pressure in mmHg. A stethoscope listens to heart/lung sounds. A manometer measures gas or fluid pressure in pipes/containers. A galvanometer detects small electric currents.

questions.text: The kilogram is unique among SI base units because it:
options: Is the only unit defined by a physical object (the IPK)
Is the only SI base unit whose name contains a prefix (kilo)
Is the heaviest base unit in value
Was the first SI unit to be redefined in 2019
questions.explanation: The kilogram is the only SI base unit whose name includes a prefix ('kilo'). All other base units (metre, second, ampere, kelvin, mole, candela) have no prefix. This means the gram is not a base unit — the kilogram is. In 2019, the kilogram was redefined in terms of Planck's constant, replacing the physical prototype (IPK) that had served since 1889.

questions.text: Which of the following is a vector quantity?
questions.explanation: Force is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction (e.g., 10 N downwards). Speed, distance, and mass are all scalar quantities (magnitude only). The vector counterparts are: velocity (speed + direction), displacement (distance + direction), and weight (mass × g, a force).

questions.text: Ammeter is used to measure electric current. It should be connected:
options: In parallel with the circuit element
In series with the circuit element
Between live and neutral wires only
questions.explanation: An ammeter measures current flowing through a circuit element and must be in SERIES so the same current passes through it. It has very low resistance to avoid disturbing the circuit. A voltmeter measures potential difference and is connected in PARALLEL (high resistance, draws negligible current).

questions.text: The dimensional formula for pressure is:
questions.explanation: Pressure = Force/Area = [MLT⁻²] / [L²] = [ML⁻¹T⁻²]. Common trap: [MLT⁻²] is Force; [ML²T⁻²] is Energy/Work; [MLT⁻¹] is Momentum. The negative exponent on L in pressure reflects that pressure decreases with area.

questions.text: Which instrument is used to measure the speed of rotation (RPM) of a machine part?
questions.explanation: A tachometer measures rotational speed in revolutions per minute (RPM) — used for engines, motors, and turbines. A speedometer measures linear vehicle speed. An odometer measures total distance travelled. An anemometer measures wind speed.
